posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Domaine August Clape is a sentimental favorite of mine. This is how I was introduced to Cornas 20 years ago with some killer vintages, including 1988 and shortly thereafter, 1995. Epiphany wines for sure. I had a '95 not too long ago, and it was stupendous.

Fast forward a decade, and these wines no longer could be found in Florida. Nobody carried them in this cultural wasteland. And worse, our parochial laws did not allow private out-of-state purchases and shipments. So I stopped buying and drinking Clape. Fast forward another series of years, and Florida relaxed its shipment laws. I started buying again. Then Parker found new loved for this once-not-so-loved (by him) estate, awarding the 2009 a 99 rating and a 2010 a 100 rating. I had loaded up on both before these lofty scores. As of yet, I have not even tried them. From these years, I was thinking 15+ years. I tried the 2010 Renassiance shortly after release and it was a wasted effort, impenetrable. I then started to pass on more recent offerings as prices spiked after the Parker ratings and perhaps the run-up with other estates like Allemand. Now I see offerings for the 2013 at prices I may be willing to swallow ($105).

What struck me as interesting - in the wake of the Parker scores on 2009 and 2010 - was first the very high praise coming from Jed Dunnuck, a very nice guy but somebody that tends to like riper wines, suggesting that this wine will mature earlier than some recent years. And then the interesting notes on CT (only two so far)

12/26/2015 - DRWINE2001 WROTE:
Almost opaque throughout. More restrained aromatically than the Renaissance with only a touch of purple flowers and blackberry jam. Deep, denser than the Renaissance, wonderful mouth coating feel, but completely unevolved; you have to trust the purity, balance, and feel rather than relying on the taste. Big, furry, persistent tannins. This should be stupendous in 2 or 3 decades, but even now, it is not too brutal for a Cornas of this age



Options
11/13/2015 - THE GUZZ WROTE: 93 Points
Amazingly intense. Nose of sweet ink, stone, ‘mud’, prune, and dark berry. Huge palate at the same time sweet and burnt fruit. Lots of extract. Like drinking dark fruit juice. Huge but interesting.

Has there been a stylistic switch or change at Clape, at least with the Cornas?

I have tried quite a few of the Vin de Amis 2009 through 2013 and have not noticed a change, but the scores and more recent notes on the Cornas picque my curiosity. If it drinks like “The Guzz” suggests, personally I would pass.

posts.name: Andreas Nielsen
posts.cooked:
Great topic Robert. I too love the wines from Clape.

I had the 2012 CdR about a month ago and it was beautiful. Cool red/blue fruit, medium bodied, fair level of tannins, a nice mouthfeel. Not at all a big wine but a very focused wine.

I didn’t detect a change in style (have also tasted the Cornas 2010 and 2011, the Renaissance 2011 and 2012 within the last couple of years) so I’ll keep buying.

Andreas

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: That just reminded me, Andreas, I had the 2011 and 2012 CdR last year as well. Had forgotten. Enjoyed them both very much. Still intrigued about the Cornas though given the scores and some notes I am seeing. I am now tempted to just go pop a 2009!

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
From a note I posted back in August:

2012 Domaine Auguste Clape Cornas - France, Rhône, Northern Rhône, Cornas (8/24/2015)
Fairly dark in color, slightly smoky with a touch of sweet fruit on the nose; rich, dark, vibrant fruit, beautiful violet note on the finish; medium tannins, a bit restrained overall for the vintage, simply gorgeous wine. What Syrah wants to be when it grows up. (94 pts.)

The good news is that if you loaded up on 09 and 10, the market is pretty strong for them (I failed to buy the 10 direct from Kermit at $83, choosing to wait and support my local retailer; then the score came out and I could only get 3 bottles at a somewhat higher price). Had the 2009 recently, it’s a nice wine, on the slightly riper end, but still damn good, particularly in a riper vintage. The one time I tasted the 2010 it was truly a black hole, dense, dark, impenetrable. I could not get a read on it, and have no idea what it will become. the 2012 is a beautiful wine. The few 13 Rhones I’ve had are, as expected based on the vintage character, a bit leaner and lighter, but still quite good. I’ve already bought a few of the 2013 Clape, I trust the estate.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
I tried that 2010 Rennaisance over your recommendation to refrain. I was a dummy. The 2010 vintage produced some monolithic wines.

My recollection is that John Morris posted a less-thrilling note on the 2012, I need to go search for it.

I am enjoying the 2013 vintage, by the way. I am ok with leaner wines, higher acid and more minerality. Sorta why I was enamored with 2011 as well.

posts.name: Andreas Nielsen
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:

That just reminded me, Andreas, I had the 2011 and 2012 CdR last year as well. Had forgotten. Enjoyed them both very much. Still intrigued about the Cornas though given the scores and some notes I am seeing. I am now tempted to just go pop a 2009!

To quote Ben Stiller: Do it!

posts.name: Andreas Nielsen
posts.cooked:
Alan Rath:

From a note I posted back in August:

2012 Domaine Auguste Clape Cornas - France, Rhône, Northern Rhône, Cornas (8/24/2015)
Fairly dark in color, slightly smoky with a touch of sweet fruit on the nose; rich, dark, vibrant fruit, beautiful violet note on the finish; medium tannins, a bit restrained overall for the vintage, simply gorgeous wine. What Syrah wants to be when it grows up. (94 pts.)

Fantastic TN - that last line really put a smile on my face.

The one time I tasted the 2010 it was truly a black hole, dense, dark, impenetrable. I could not get a read on it, and have no idea what it will become.

Had the same experience when I tasted it. Didn’t provide any drinking pleasure at that time. Think it needs at last 8-10 years to shine.

Did any of you guys taste the 2011 Cornas? IIRC it is rated quite a bit better than the 2012 on CT.

I was partly correct:

WineBerserkers – 7 Feb 15

TN: 2012 N Rhones (Clape, Gonon, Texier and more)

My regular blind tasting group met on Wednesday to taste 2012 Northern Rhones. With the exception of the Clape, there were no heavy hitters, but the wines in the main flight were good across the board, and some offered good value. Three – the...

You had it, posted a decent note and moderate score. But, you forgot about it, so can’t be good, right?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: 2010 seemed correct to me. Don’t really understand the sudden slew of scores for a wine that had been exceptional for decades. I would personally approach prognostications of early drinkability with some skepticism, and it sounds you have significant experience of your own to extrapolate from!

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:
John Morris:
Robert Alfert:

My recollection is that John Morris posted a less-thrilling note on the 2012, I need to go search for it.

Then you have a better memory than me. I’ll have to dig out my notes.

I was partly correct:

TN: 2012 N Rhones (Clape, Gonon, Texier and more) - WINE TALK - WineBerserkers

You had it, posted a decent note and moderate score. But, you forgot about it, so can’t be good, right?

The 2012 tied for first place for me and I gave it 92+++ on day three, which is a very high score for me.

posts.name: Carlos Delpin
posts.cooked:
I think all of that weedy cab franc is interfering with your ability to enjoy powerful manly wines. Cornas in general needs time and Clape, Voge, and Allemand require more patience than most. The 2010 Clape Cornas has deep, brooding, powerful fruit but also packs the Cornas tipicity that we all love. I don’t think that the style has changed. My take is that the 2010 vintage was hard to control and in some wines the power took over.

Mis dos centavos licenciado.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Andreas Nielsen:

Did any of you guys taste the 2011 Cornas? IIRC it is rated quite a bit better than the 2012 on CT.

Andreas,
I don’t seem to have tasted the 2011 Cornas, but have had the 2011 Renaissance a couple of times, and it is a really nice wine. I’ve quite liked most of the 2011 Rhones I’ve had the chance to drink or try, but in my own experience it seems to be a vintage that will not last quite as long as a really good vintage like 2010, 2012 (or, in particular, 2005 which is the vintage of that decade I most like).

posts.name: Andreas Nielsen
posts.cooked:
Alan Rath:
Andreas Nielsen:

Did any of you guys taste the 2011 Cornas? IIRC it is rated quite a bit better than the 2012 on CT.

Andreas,
I don’t seem to have tasted the 2011 Cornas, but have had the 2011 Renaissance a couple of times, and it is a really nice wine. I’ve quite liked most of the 2011 Rhones I’ve had the chance to drink or try, but in my own experience it seems to be a vintage that will not last quite as long as a really good vintage like 2010, 2012 (or, in particular, 2005 which is the vintage of that decade I most like).

Alan,
I also tasted the 2011 Renaissance and remember enjoying it (didn’t write a TN) but I also really enjoy young wine. Normally I open one of the Renaissance quite soon after receiving my allocation. Sadly I never had a Clape older than the 2010 since I didn’t learn about this domaine until a few years ago. I bet the 2005s are good - had a flight of Chave Hermitage (2003, 2005 and 2007) about three years ago and enjoyed the 2005 the most.

posts.name: Daniel Moritz
posts.cooked: Holy smokes, I had the 2010 Clape Cornas last night. Absolutely ridiculously good. I told my wife to pull something from the cellar and let me blind taste. Without even tasting it, just smelling it in the decanter, I guessed 2010 Cornas, though I guessed Allemand…my bad! The nose was intoxicating, so animalistic and meaty and delicious. Could make a cologne from it! Amazing wine on the palate. It was decanted for about an hour and a half before drinking. Just had to post, killer wine. Glad I bought these before the reviews came out!

posts.name: Daniel Moritz
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:

Wow, thanks for posting that note!

I’m still thinking about it this morning!
